subject: ap-government
title: SCOTUS Comparison — Betts v. Brady (1942)
stimulus:
In 1941, Smith Betts was arrested for robbery in the state of Maryland. Unable to afford a lawyer, he was denied a public defender by a judge and forced to represent himself. He was found guilty and given an eight-year sentence.

In the ensuing case, Betts v. Brady (1942), the Supreme Court ruled against Mr. Betts, who claimed his constitutional right to a fair trial was denied. In a 6-3 decision, the Court held that the right to counsel applied to only criminal cases in federal courts and that he did not have a right to counsel in a state court, except in death penalty cases.

Justice Black, in his dissenting opinion, reasoned, "This case can be determined by a resolution of a narrower question: whether, in view of the nature of the offense and the circumstances of his trial and conviction, this petitioner was denied the procedural protection which is his right under the federal Constitution. I think he was."

parts.prompt: Identify the constitutional amendment in the Bill of Rights that is common to both Gideon v. Wainwright (1963) and Betts v. Brady (1942).
parts.scoring_notes: Only the Sixth Amendment earns the point. The Fifth or Fourteenth Amendment does not earn the point for this question.

scoring_points.description: Identify the constitutional amendment in the Bill of Rights that is common to both Gideon v. Wainwright (1963) and Betts v. Brady (1942).

required_elements: Identifies the Sixth Amendment as the constitutional amendment common to both cases
alternatives.correct_example: The Sixth Amendment is the constitutional amendment common to both Gideon v. Wainwright and Betts v. Brady.

wrong_examples: The Fifth Amendment — while due process applies, the specific right to counsel is guaranteed by the Sixth Amendment.
The Fourteenth Amendment — this was the vehicle for incorporating the Sixth Amendment to the states, but the amendment at the core of both cases is the Sixth Amendment.
common_traps: Accept 'Sixth Amendment right to counsel' or 'right to an attorney' as equivalent
Do not accept the Fourteenth Amendment alone — the question asks for the Bill of Rights amendment

parts.prompt: Explain how the decision in Gideon v. Wainwright relates to the reasoning in Justice Black's dissenting opinion in Betts v. Brady.
parts.scoring_notes: First point requires accurate information about Gideon v. Wainwright (the required case). Second point requires explaining how Black's dissent in Betts anticipated/influenced the Gideon decision — Black argued in dissent that the Constitution required right to counsel in state courts, and the Gideon Court later adopted exactly that reasoning.

scoring_points.description: Explain how Gideon relates to Black's dissent in Betts. (1 point — describe relevant fact or holding about the required Supreme Court case Gideon v. Wainwright)

required_elements: Accurately describes the holding or facts from Gideon v. Wainwright, the required case
alternatives.correct_example: In Gideon v. Wainwright, the Supreme Court unanimously held that the Sixth Amendment's right to counsel applies to state courts, meaning states must provide an attorney to criminal defendants who cannot afford one.

required_elements: Accurately describes the holding or facts from Gideon v. Wainwright, the required case
alternatives.correct_example: Clarence Gideon, who was denied a court-appointed attorney in a Florida burglary case, represented himself, was convicted, and later petitioned the Supreme Court. The Court ruled in his favor, requiring states to provide counsel to indigent defendants.

wrong_examples: Describes only Betts without mentioning Gideon — earns 0 points.
Confuses Gideon with Miranda v. Arizona or another due process case.
common_traps: Only Gideon information is needed for the first point
Both the facts and the holding earn the point

scoring_points.description: Explain how Gideon relates to Black's dissent in Betts. (2 points — correctly explains how both connect)

required_elements: Explains that Justice Black's dissent in Betts argued the Constitution required right to counsel in state courts, and that the Gideon Court later adopted that same reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: In his Betts dissent, Justice Black argued that the U.S. Constitution required all states to provide counsel to defendants in serious criminal cases — that the Sixth Amendment should apply to state courts. When the Court decided Gideon more than twenty years later, it adopted Justice Black's dissenting reasoning and overruled Betts, holding that the right to counsel is fundamental and must be provided in state criminal proceedings.

wrong_examples: States that 'Black agreed with Gideon' without explaining the connection between the dissent's reasoning and the later ruling.
Describes only Gideon without mentioning Black's dissent or the connection between them.
common_traps: Must address BOTH — the Betts dissent and the Gideon holding — to earn the second point
The key connection is that Black's Betts dissent effectively predicted and anticipated the constitutional reasoning the Court would adopt in Gideon

parts.prompt: Explain how the decision in Betts v. Brady demonstrates the principle of federalism.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain the connection between the Betts ruling and federalism — specifically that the Court's refusal to incorporate the Sixth Amendment to states preserved state power to define their own court procedures. The response must explain the federalism connection, not just mention the word.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the decision in Betts v. Brady demonstrates the principle of federalism.

required_elements: Explains that by not incorporating the Sixth Amendment to states, the Betts ruling preserved state authority over their own criminal court procedures
Connects this to the federalism principle of divided sovereignty between state and federal governments
alternatives.correct_example: Federalism divides power between the national and state governments. The Betts decision demonstrates federalism because by ruling that states are not required to provide counsel in criminal trials, the Court left states free to define their own court procedures. This preserved the states' reserved powers under the Tenth Amendment to govern their own judicial systems without being required to follow the federal standard.

required_elements: Explains how Betts preserved state autonomy in criminal procedure, reflecting the federalism principle that states retain reserved powers
alternatives.correct_example: In Betts, the Supreme Court declined to incorporate the Sixth Amendment's right to counsel against the states, demonstrating federalism because the decision recognized that states, not the federal government, had the authority to determine when defendants in state criminal cases are entitled to appointed counsel.

wrong_examples: Simply stating 'Betts involved both state and federal courts' without explaining the federalism principle.
Describing selective incorporation without connecting it to the federalism concept of divided state and federal power.
common_traps: The response must specifically explain how the Betts ruling preserved state power — not just mention 'federalism'
Note that Gideon later overturned Betts — but the question asks about Betts, so focus on how it demonstrated federalism at the time it was decided
